# **Skeletal System: Appendicular division**

![](images/_page_195_Picture_1.jpeg)

**Figure 8.1** The Appendicular Skeleton

### **Your objectives for this lab are to:**

- Identify the major divisions of the skeleton, axial vs appendicular, and which bones belong to each group.
- Provide examples of each type of bone classification by shape
- Identify disarticulated bones as left or right. Be able to describe how you made this determination.
- Identify the 24 bones and select bone markings on articulated skeletons (or portions of the skeleton) and disarticulated bones. The list below specifies the appendicular bones you need to know, and the specific bone markings required for each bone.

## **Terms**

## **Pectoral Girdle and Upper Extremities**

- Clavicle:
  - o Sternal end
  - o Acromial end
- Scapula,
  - o Spine
  - o Acromion process
  - o Coracoid process
  - o Glenoid fossa/cavity
- Humerus
  - o Greater tubercle
  - o Lesser tubercle
  - o Head
  - o Coronoid fossa
  - o Olecranon fossa
  - o Trochlea
  - o Capitulum

- o Medial epicondyle
- o Lateral epicondyle
- Ulna
- o Olecranon process,
- o Trochlear process
- o Coronoid process
- o Radial notch
- o Styloid process (medial)
- Radius
  - o Styloid process (lateral)
  - o Head
  - o Radial tuberosity
- Hand
- o Carpals
- o Metacarpals
- o Phalanges

## **Pelvic Girdle and Lower Extremities**

- Pelvic bones (Os Coxae)
  - o Ilium,
  - o Ischium
  - o Pubis/pubic bone
  - o Iliac crest
  - o Obturator foramen
  - o Greater sciatic arch
  - o Acetabulum
  - o Pubic Arch
  - o Pubic symphysis
  - o Ischial tuberosity
- Femur
- o Head
- o Neck
- o Greater trochanter
- o Lesser trochanter
- o Medial and lateral condyles

- Tibia
- o Medial and lateral condyles
- o Tibial tuberosity
- o Anterior crest
- o Medial malleolus
- Fibula
  - o Head
  - o Lateral malleolus
- Patella
- Foot
- o Tarsals
- o talus,
- o calcaneus,
- o cuboid,
- o navicular,
- o cuneiform bones
  - medial
  - intermediate
  - lateral
- o Metatarsals
- o Phalanges

# **Prelab Activities**

# **Prelab Activity 8.1: Definitions**

# Appendicular Skeleton

| •      | Pectoral girdle    |
|--------|--------------------|
| •      | Clavicle           |
| •      | Scapula            |
| •      | Humerus            |
| •      | Radius             |
| •      | Ulna               |
| •      | Carpals            |
| •      | Metacarpals        |
| •      | Digits (phalanges) |
| Pelvio | : Girdle           |
| 1.     | Os Coxa            |
| 2.     | Femur              |
|        | Tibia              |
| 4.     | Fibula             |
|        | Tarsals            |
| 6.     | Metatarsals        |
|        | Phalanges (digits) |
| Other  | Definitions        |
| 1.     | Ligaments          |
| 2.     | tendons            |

## **Prelab Activity 8.2**

Label the appendicular skeletal bones.

![](images/_page_198_Picture_2.jpeg)

**Figure 8.2** Unlabeled Human Skeleton.

## **Appendicular Skeleton**

- Review the material in your text on the two divisions of the skeletal system.
- Form your assigned groups and obtain a skeleton and a set of disarticulated bones from the storage area.
- Identify and label the bones found in the appendicular skeleton.
- Have your instructor review your work for correctness.
- Then take a picture of your finished work for later review.

![](images/_page_199_Picture_7.jpeg)

**Figure 8.3** The Appendicular Skeleton.

![](images/_page_200_Figure_0.jpeg)

**Figure 8.4** Bones of the Appendicular Skeleton (Anterior View).

**Figure 8.5** Bones of the appendicular skeleton (Posterior View).

## **Pectoral Girdle**

## **Lab Activity 8.2**

- Review the material in your text on the appendicular division of the skeletal system.
- Obtain a box of disarticulated bones from the storage area.
- Identify and label the bones found in the pectoral girdle of the appendicular skeleton.
- Have your instructor review your work for correctness.
- Then take a picture of your finished work for later review.

- Clavicle:
  - o Sternal end
  - o Acromial end

- Scapula,
  - o Spine
  - o Acromion process
  - o Coracoid process
  - o Glenoid fossa/cavity

![](images/_page_201_Picture_16.jpeg)

**Figure 8.6** Anterior and Posterior View of the Pectoral Girdle.

## **The Clavicles**

![](images/_page_202_Picture_1.jpeg)

**Figure 8.7** The Clavicle. Top view shows the anterior side of the left clavicle; Bottom view shows the posterior side of the left clavicle.

## **The Scapula**

![](images/_page_202_Picture_4.jpeg)

![](images/_page_202_Picture_5.jpeg)

**Figure 8.8** The Scapula (Anterior and Posterior Aspects).

## **Bones of the Upper Limb (Arm)**

## **Lab Activity 8.3**

- Break up into your assigned groups. Obtain an articulated skeleton and a box of disarticulated bones from the classroom lab supplies.
- Use the information from your text and the following pages to locate and identify the bones of the upper limb and each bone's markings (surface features).
- Use some labelling tape to identify each bone's markings and have another group check your work for correctness. Have your instructor review your material as well.
- Remember to take some pictures of your work to include in your study guide.

## **Terms**

#### • **Humerus**

- o Greater tubercle
- o Lesser tubercle
- o Head
- o Coronoid fossa
- o Olecranon fossa

- o Trochlea
- o Capitulum
- o Medial epicondyle
- o Lateral epicondyle

![](images/_page_203_Picture_17.jpeg)

**Figure 8.9** Humerus (Posterior View).

![](images/_page_204_Picture_0.jpeg)

Figure 8.10 The Humerus and Elbow.

## **Terms**

- **Ulnar**
  - o Olecranon process,
  - o Trochlear process
  - o Coronoid process
  - o Radial notch
  - o Styloid process (medial)

## • **Radius**

- o Styloid process (lateral)
- o Head
- o Radial tuberosity

![](images/_page_205_Picture_11.jpeg)

**Figure 8.11** Ulna and Radius.

- **Hand**
  - o Carpals o Metacarpals o Phalanges

![](images/_page_206_Picture_3.jpeg)

**Figure 8.12a** Anatomy of the Hand (Anterior View).

![](images/_page_206_Picture_5.jpeg)

**Figure 8.12b** Anatomy of the Hand.

## **The Lower limb (leg)**

## **Pelvic Girdle**

### **Lab Activity 8.5**

- Break up into your assigned groups. Obtain an articulated skeleton and a box of bones from the classroom lab supplies.
- Use the information from your text and the following pages to locate and identify the bones of the pelvic girdle and each bone's markings (surface features).
- Use some labelling tape to identify each bone's markings and have another group check your work for correctness. Have your instructor review your material as well.
- Remember to take some pictures of your work to include in your study guide.

- Pelvic girdle Pelvic bones (Os Coxae)
  - o Ilium,
  - o Ischium
  - o Pubis/pubic bone

- Features
  - o Iliac crest
  - o Obturator foramen
  - o Greater sciatic arch
  - o Acetabulum
  - o Pubic Arch
  - o Pubic symphysis
  - o Ischial tuberosity

#### Os Coxa llium lliac fossa lliac crest Posterior Anterior · Posterior superior superior superior iliac spine iliac spine iliac spine Posterior Anterior inferior Posterior • inferior iliac spine inferior iliac spine iliac spine Acetabulum Auricular surface Greater sciatic Arcuate line -Greater sciatic notch Superior ramus of pubis notch Ischial body -Ischial spine Pubic tubercle -Lesser sciatic Ischial spine -Pubic body notch Lesser sciatic --Pubis Obturator notch Articular surface foramen Ischium of pubis (at pubic Ischium symphysis) Obturator -Ischial ramus foramen Inferior ramus Ischial tuberosity of pubis

Lateral view, right hip bone Figure 8.13 Hip Bone (Os Coxa).

Ischial ramus

Ischiopubic ramus

Medial view, right hip bone

Ischiopubic ramus

209 | Page

## **Pelvis**

![](images/_page_209_Picture_1.jpeg)

Figure 8.14 The Pelvis.

## Pelvises: Female and Male

![](images/_page_209_Picture_4.jpeg)

**Figure 8.15** Female and Male Pelvic Girdles.

## **The Lower Limb (leg)**

## **Lab Activity 8.6**

- Break up into your assigned groups. Obtain an articulated skeleton and a box of bones from the classroom lab supplies.
- Use the information from your text and the following pages to locate and identify the bones of the lower limb (leg) and each bone's markings (surface features).
- Use some labelling tape to identify each bone's markings and have another group check your work for correctness. Have your instructor review your material as well.
- Remember to take some pictures of your work to include in your study guide.

### Terms

- Femur
  - o Head
  - o Neck
  - o Greater trochanter
  - o Lesser trochanter
  - o Medial and lateral condyles

• Patella

![](images/_page_211_Picture_0.jpeg)

Figure 8.16 Femur and Patella.

## **Terms**

Tibia

- o Medial and lateral condyles
- o Tibial tuberosity
- o Anterior crest
- o Medial malleolus

Fibula

- o Head
- o Lateral malleolus

![](images/_page_212_Picture_9.jpeg)

**Figure 8.17** Tibia and Fibula.

## **Foot**

- Tarsals
  - o talus,
  - o calcaneus,
  - o cuboid,
  - o navicular,
  - o cuneiform bones
    - medial
    - intermediate
    - lateral

- Metatarsals
- Phalanges

![](images/_page_213_Picture_12.jpeg)

**Figure 8.18** Bones of the Foot.

## **Additional Learning Resources**

**Watch** these videos to learn about bones and markings of the appendicular skeleton:

- Bones of the upper extremity: <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7v2pwRwyWqU>
- Bones of the lower extremity: <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5ohJGtD3vRY>
- Bones of the pelvis:<https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W8i7jlFa7qc>

**Watch** these videos to help you identify left vs right for appendicular bones:

- <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0KPR9NSuGOA&feature=youtu.be> **(most appendicular bones)**
- <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kKGeEcrTMg8> **(ilium, ischium, pubis)**
- <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dVzlkGmm4Fg> **(scapula)**

**More Practice** identifying bones and markings with these websites:

- <https://webanatomy.umn.edu/various-bones>
- <https://webanatomy.umn.edu/skulls-skeletons>
- <https://pierceanatomy.weebly.com/>
- <https://www.getbodysmart.com/skeletal-system>
- <http://www.personal.psu.edu/faculty/j/a/jas43/skel/skeletal.htm>
- this site [\(http://openstax.org/l/handbone\)](http://openstax.org/l/handbone) to explore the bones and joints of the hand.
- Watch this video [\(http://openstax.org/l/3Dpelvis\)](http://openstax.org/l/3Dpelvis) for a 3-D view of the pelvis and its associated ligaments. What is the large opening in the bony pelvis, located between the ischium and pubic regions, and what two parts of the pubis contribute to the formation of this opening?
- Use this tutorial [\(http://openstax.org/l/footbones\)](http://openstax.org/l/footbones) to review the bones of the foot. Which tarsal bones are in the proximal, intermediate, and distal groups?
- Visit this site [\(http://openstax.org/l/kneesurgery\)](http://openstax.org/l/kneesurgery) to watch a virtual knee replacement surgery. The prosthetic knee components must be properly aligned to function properly. How is this alignment ensured?

# **Testing your understanding**

**Post Lab Activity 8.1: Labeling**

**Label all of the following figures of the bones.**

Label the appendicular skeletal bones.

![](images/_page_215_Picture_4.jpeg)

**Figure 8.19** The Human Skeleton.

Identify and label seven (7) features of this bone (anterior view)

![](images/_page_216_Picture_1.jpeg)

**Figure 8.20** Anterior View of the Humerus.

Identify and label all of the bones and their features of this structure.

![](images/_page_216_Picture_4.jpeg)

**Figure 8.21** The Pelvic Girdle **(Anterior View).**

## **Post Lab Activity 8.2: Crossword Puzzles**

Complete the crossword puzzle.

![](images/_page_217_Figure_2.jpeg)

**Figure 18.22** Appendicular skeleton.

| Across                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | •                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| • 6 The, formed by all three bones that make up the coxal bone, articulates with the femur. (10) • 7 The lateral end of the clavicle articulates with the of the (8,7) • 8 The shoulder girdle consists of the and the (8,9) • 11 The is formed by the two hip bones, the sacrum, and the coccyx. (6) • 12 The medial end of the clavicle articulates with the (7) • Down | 2 The superior surface of the of the articulates with the capitulum of the humerus. (4,6) • 3 The of the scapula articulates with the humerus. (7,6) • 4 The hip bones are called the (5,5) • 5 Three bones fuse to form each coxal bone. The superior portion of the coxal bone is formed by the (5) • 9 The heads of the metacarpals articulate with the (9) • 10 The forms the posteroinferior portion of the coxal bone. (7) |
| • 1 The upper limbs are attached to                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |

the trunk by the \_\_\_\_\_ girdle (8)

## **Answer keys**

## **Appendicular Skeleton**

**Across: 6** Acetabulum, **7** Acromion scapula, **8** Scapulae clavicles, **11** Pelvis, **12** 

Sternum.

**Down: 1** Pectoral, **2** Head radius, **3** Glenoid cavity, **4** Coxal bones, **5** Ilium, **9** 

Phalanges, **10** Ischium.
